items.html:
The laws behind safeguarding children

Two Acts set the legal ground for protecting children in England. The Children Act 1989 established that a child’s welfare is the paramount consideration when a court decides questions about their upbringing. It defines when a child is ‘in need’, and it places a duty on local authorities to investigate where there is reasonable cause to suspect that a child is suffering, or likely to suffer, significant harm. In law, a child is anyone who has not yet reached their eighteenth birthday.

The Children Act 2004 built on this after reviews showed agencies working in isolation. It strengthened the duty on organisations to work together and to make arrangements that safeguard and promote the welfare of children.

Working Together and everyone’s responsibility

Working Together to Safeguard Children is the statutory guidance that puts these duties into practice, and its 2023 edition is clear that safeguarding is everyone’s responsibility. No single agency can keep a child safe alone, and every worker who comes into contact with children and families has a part to play.

  • Safeguarding partners: the local authority, the police and the integrated care board share leadership for children in their area.
  • Your part: you are not expected to investigate, but you are expected to notice, to be curious, and to pass concerns on.
  • Health roles: if you work in a health role you also follow the intercollegiate guidance from the Royal College of Nursing and the Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health, trained to the level that matches your role.

items.question: Under the Children Act 1989, what must be the paramount consideration when a court makes decisions about a child’s upbringing?

answers.feedback: Correct. The 1989 Act makes the child’s welfare the paramount consideration in decisions about their upbringing.

answers.text: The wishes of the child’s parents.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. A parent’s wishes are considered, but the child’s welfare comes first.

answers.text: The cost to the local authority.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Cost does not override a child’s welfare, which is the paramount consideration.

answers.text: The reputation of the agencies involved.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Agency reputation is never the deciding factor; the child’s welfare is paramount.

items.html:
Signs of physical abuse and neglect

You already know the four categories of abuse. Recognising them in real life means noticing signs that do not add up. Possible indicators of physical abuse include injuries in unusual places, bruises in the shape of a hand or an object, injuries that do not match the explanation given, or a child who flinches at contact. One sign on its own rarely proves anything; it is the pattern and the context that matter.

Neglect — the ongoing failure to meet a child’s basic needs — may show as a child who is often hungry, dirty or dressed unsuitably for the weather, who has untreated medical or dental problems, or who is repeatedly left without proper supervision.

Signs of emotional and sexual abuse

Emotional abuse can be the hardest to see because it leaves no marks. You might notice a child who is withdrawn or overly anxious, who seems fearful of a parent, who is desperate for attention, or whose confidence and development lag without a clear reason.

Sexual abuse indicators can be physical or behavioural: pain or discomfort, sexual knowledge or behaviour beyond what you would expect for the child’s age, or a sudden change such as becoming secretive or afraid of a particular person. Children are rarely able to say plainly what is wrong, so changes in mood, behaviour or routine are often the first clue. Trust what you observe, write it down, and share it — you do not need to be certain to raise a concern.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete these key points about recognising abuse.
items.textField: A *pattern* of injuries that do not match the explanation may point to physical abuse. Neglect is the ongoing failure to meet a child’s basic *needs*, such as food, warmth and supervision. Emotional abuse often leaves no *marks*, showing instead as fear or withdrawal. Sexual knowledge beyond a child’s *age* can be an indicator of sexual abuse. You do not have to be *certain* before you raise a concern.

items.html:
Exploitation: county lines and CSE

Some children are harmed by people outside their family who deliberately exploit them. Child criminal exploitation includes ‘county lines’, where criminal groups use children to store and sell drugs, often moving them away from home. Signs can include a child with unexplained money, phones or clothing, going missing, or seeming frightened of older ‘friends’.

Child sexual exploitation is a form of sexual abuse in which a child is manipulated or coerced into sexual activity, sometimes in return for gifts, affection or status. Exploited children are often groomed into believing they are willing participants, so they may not see themselves as victims — which is exactly why the adults around them must stay alert.

Online harm, FGM and radicalisation

Harm increasingly reaches children through screens. Online risks include grooming, bullying, and exposure to harmful or extremist material.

  • FGM: female genital mutilation is the partial or total removal of external female genitalia for non-medical reasons. It is illegal in the UK and is child abuse; warning signs may include talk of a ‘special procedure’ or a long trip abroad.
  • Radicalisation: the process by which someone comes to support terrorism or extremist ideologies. Prevent is the part of the Government’s counter-terrorism strategy that aims to stop people being drawn into terrorism.
  • Your response: in every case you notice and report through the usual safeguarding routes — it is not for you to decide whether the risk is real.

items.question: An adult you support has a teenage grandson who is often at the home. He has begun going missing for days, has a second phone and trainers no one can explain, and seems frightened of older men who wait outside. What might this suggest?

answers.text: Possible child criminal exploitation, such as county lines, which you should report.
answers.feedback: Correct. Going missing, unexplained items and fear of older figures are recognised signs of criminal exploitation, and you should report them.

answers.text: Normal teenage behaviour that is none of your concern.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. These signs together may point to exploitation, and safeguarding a child is always your concern.

answers.text: That he is simply doing well and earning his own money.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Unexplained money and phones alongside going missing and fear are warning signs, not signs of success.

answers.text: A matter to raise directly with the older men outside.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Confronting the people involved could place the child and you at risk; report through safeguarding routes instead.

items.html:
Early help and thresholds

Not every worry means a child is at immediate risk, and the right response is matched to the level of need. Where a child and family would benefit from support before problems grow, ‘early help’ offers coordinated assistance — for example around housing, parenting or a child’s behaviour. Where a child may be in need or suffering significant harm, a referral to children’s social care is needed.

Local safeguarding partners publish threshold guidance describing what level of concern calls for what kind of help. You are not expected to judge the exact threshold yourself. Your task is to describe clearly and honestly what you have seen and heard, and to pass it to the people whose role is to decide.

MASH and what happens next

Many areas run a Multi-Agency Safeguarding Hub, or MASH, where the local authority, police, health and other partners share information about a child. This helps them build a fuller picture quickly and agree the right response.

After you report a concern, it may be screened, information may be gathered from other agencies, and a decision is made: no further action, early help, an assessment of a child in need, or — where significant harm is suspected — a child protection enquiry under section 47 of the Children Act 1989. If a child is in immediate danger you do not wait: you call 999. You may not always hear the outcome, but your report still matters.

items.question: As an adult social care worker, it is your job to decide exactly which threshold a concern meets before you report it.
items.correctMsg: Correct. You are not expected to judge the threshold. Your job is to describe clearly what you have seen and heard and pass it to those whose role is to decide.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. Deciding the threshold is the role of children’s social care. You simply report your concerns clearly and let them decide the right response.

items.html:
Your role and its boundaries

You are employed to support adults, but your duty to safeguard children does not switch off when you step into a service user’s home. Children may be present as sons, daughters, grandchildren or visitors, and you may be one of the few outsiders who sees them. Knowing your boundaries keeps children safe and keeps you acting professionally.

Your role is to notice, to record factually, and to report through your organisation’s safeguarding lead and local procedures. It is not your role to investigate, to question a child at length, to confront a parent, or to decide whether abuse has happened. Never promise a child that you will keep what they tell you a secret — explain kindly that you may need to tell someone who can help.

A realistic scenario

You visit Mr Adeyemi twice a week. His seven-year-old granddaughter, Grace, is often there. Over several visits you notice she is thin, wears the same unwashed clothes, flinches when her father raises his voice, and one day says quietly, “I’m not allowed to tell you about home.”

You feel unsure — Grace is not the person you were sent to support. Even so, you stay calm, you do not press her for detail or promise secrecy, and you reassure her that she was right to talk to you. As soon as you leave you write down her exact words and what you saw, and you report the same day to your safeguarding lead so children’s social care can consider what help Grace needs.

items.question: In the scenario, what is the right thing to do about your concerns for Grace?

answers.text: Stay calm, avoid promising secrecy, record her exact words, and report to your safeguarding lead the same day.
answers.feedback: Correct. Calm listening, factual recording and prompt reporting let children’s social care consider what help Grace needs.

answers.text: Say nothing, because you were sent to support Mr Adeyemi, not Grace.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Your duty to safeguard children applies to any child you encounter, not only the person you were sent to support.

answers.text: Question Grace closely to find out exactly what is happening at home.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Investigating is not your role; pressing a child for detail can cause harm and complicate any enquiry.

answers.text: Warn Grace’s father that you have concerns about how he speaks to her.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Confronting a parent may place Grace at greater risk; report through safeguarding routes instead.
